0: Anaerobic digestion and milking frequency as mitigation strategies of the environmental burden in the milk production system
Jacopo Bacenetti, Luciana Bava, Maddalena Zucali * Daniela Lovarelli, Anna Sandrucci, Alberto Tamburini, Marco Fiala
Department of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences, Production, Landscape, Agroenergy, Università degli Studi di Milano, Via G. Celoria 2, 20133 Milan, Italy
1:
<list>The environmental impact of milk in Italy was estimated through Life Cycle Assessment. 
Livestock and crop production had a great effect on environmental impact of dairy farms. 
Third milking and anaerobic digestion (AD) were assessed as mitigation strat- legies. 
AD strongly reduces acidification, global warming, eutrophication and energy use. 
Environmental benefits were found in- creasing milking frequency from 2 to 3 per day.</list>

0: Environmental burden in the milk production system
1: <header>A R T I C L INFO </header>
Article history: Received 9 July 2015 Received in revised form 3 September 2015 Accepted 3 September 2015 Available online 15 September 2015
Keywords: Milk Life Cycle Assessment Dairy farm Biogas Milking frequency
2: The aim of the study was to assess, through a cradle to farm gate Life Cycle Assessment, different mitigation strat- legies of the potential environmental impacts of milk production at farm level. The environmental performances of a conventional intensive dairy farm in Northern Italy (baseline scenario) were compared with the results ob- tained: from the introduction of the third daily milking and from the adoption of anaerobic digestion (AD) of an- imal slurry in a consortium AD plant. The AD plant, fed only with animal slurries coming also from nearby farms. Key parameters concerning on-farm activities (forage production, energy consumptions, agricultural machines maintenance, manure and livestock management), off-farm activities (production of fertilizers, pesticides, bed- ding materials, purchased forages, purchased concentrate feed, replacement animals, agricultural machines manufacturing, electricity, fuel) and transportation were considered. The functional unit was 1 kg fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) leaving the farm gate. The selected environmental impact categories were: global warming potential, acidification, eutrophication, photochemical oxidation and non-renewable energy use. The production of 1 kg of FPCM caused, in the baseline scenario, the following environmental impact potentials: glob- al warming potential 1.12 kg CO2 eq; acidification 15.5 g SO2 eq; eutrophication 5.62 g PO eq; photochemical oxidation 0.87 g C2H4 eq/kg FPCM; energy use 4.66 MJ eq. The increase of milking frequency improved environ- mental performances for all impact categories in comparison with the baseline scenario; in particular acidifica- tion and eutrophication potentials showed the largest reductions (-11 and - 12%, respectively). In anaerobic

digestion scenario, compared to the baseline one, most of the impact potentials were strongly reduced. In partic- ular the most important advantages were in terms of acidification -29%), global warming (-22%) and eutro- phication potential (-18%). The AD of cow slurry is confirmed as an effective strategy to mitigate the environmental impact of milk production at farm level.

4: The contribution to the global human-induced greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from milk production has been recently estimated at about 3% (Gerber et al., 2013).

The European dairy farming is currently facing numerous changes; in particular a progressive intensification process has largely increased crop and animal productivity with the introduction of new technolo- gies, process specialization, large scale mechanization, and increased use of external inputs (Gaudino et al., 2014). Intensification of milk pro- duction requests an increase of the use of external input in the dairy farm with a consequence concentration of gas emissions and nutrient flow in the environment at local scale. Moreover, new green payments across farms within the latest Common Agricultural Policy reform and the removal of the European Union (EU) milk quota system in 2015 are expected to lead to further changes for EU dairy farmers (Del Prado et al., 2013).

A number of strategies for mitigating the environmental impact of milk production at farm level have been proposed. Maximizing produc- tion efficiency at animal level seems to produce the best results in terms of environmental impact (Kristensen et al., 2011; De Boer et al., 2011; Rotz et al., 2010), particularly by: increasing of genetic merit of the herd (O'Brien et al., 2014); administering BSt (Rotz et al., 2010); reduc- ing the replacement rate (Vellinga et al., 2011); improving fertility and health parameters (De Boer et al., 2011); finding the optimal combina- tion between milk yield and replacement rate (Zehetmeier et al., 2014); increasing efficiency of feed conversion through balanced and precise feeding and selection of high producing cows (Guerci et al., 2013); im- proving ensiling (Bacenetti and Fusi, 2015).

According to Weiske et al. (2006), besides improving production ef- ficiency, other promising actions for mitigating the impacts of dairy farms are: 1) frequent removal of slurry and use of scraping systems; 2) improved slurry storage and 3) biogas production by anaerobic di- gestion (AD).Traditionally, in Italy, the intensive dairy farms use to milk cows twice a day. The introduction of the third milking, with the consequent increase of milk production (Erdman and Varner, 1995; Smith et al. (2002), seems to be an interesting strategy in order to in- crease the profitability of dairy farms but can also have positive effects in reducing the environmental impact per milk unit.

On the other hand, anaerobic digestion (AD) of animal slurry has been recognized by several studies (Bacenetti et al., 2013; Lijó et al., 2014a; Lijó et al., 2014b) as a suitable and effective solution to produce energy and to reduce the environmental impact (in particular GHG emissions) of livestock activities. In Italy, thanks to strong public incen- tives for electricity (EE) generation from renewable sources, there are about 1150 AD plants (Negri et al., 2014a). Although most of these plants are fed with cereal silages (González-García et al., 2013; Bacenetti et al., 2014), after the revision of the subsidies framework (Ministero Sviluppo Economico, 2012) the interest about small AD plants (electric power < 300 kW) fed with animal slurries is increasing (Negri et al., 2014b).

Nowadays, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is also accepted and used for the evaluation of agricultural activities, where it can be applied to: 1) de- tect the environmental hotspots (processes or activities responsible for the main share of the environmental impacts) and, 2) to compare differ- ent processes or different technical solutions that can be implemented in the same process (Harada et al., 2007; González-García et al., 2013; Bacenetti et al., 2013; Bacenetti et al., 2015b). The European Commis- sion states that "LCAs provide the best framework for assessing the
potential environmental impacts of products currently available" 2003).

The aim of the study was to assess through an attributional LCA ap- proach the potential environmental impacts of milk production at farm level under different mitigation strategies. In particular the environ- mental performances of a conventional intensive dairy farm in Northern Italy (baseline scenario) were compared with the results obtained: 1) from the introduction of the third daily milking 2) from the adoption of anaerobic digestion of animal slurry thanks to a consortium plant.

9: The impact potential of milk production in the two strategies was evaluated with a Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) approach using as a model an intensive dairy farm located in the Po Valley area (Northern Italy), and, more precisely, on the experimental farm of the University of Milan at Landriano (PV). The herd is composed by 85 lactating cows, 25 dry cows, 64 heifers (between 6 and 24 months) and 18 female calves.
The global farm agricultural area is 50 ha; all the surface can be irrigated.
The local climate is characterized by an average annual temperature of 12.7 °C, and the rainfall is mainly concentrated in autumn and spring (average annual precipitation 745 mm).
This study has been carried out from a cradle-to-farm gate perspec- tive. The LCA model was carried out by including three subsystems: 1) crop cultivation, 2) livestock and 3) slurry storage.

The subsystem 1 involves the cultivation of maize (1 hybrid for whole plant silage production), winter cereals (mix of wheat and tritica- le), Italian ryegrass and sorghum. The subsystem 1 could be considered as upstream process, as defined by PCR rules for raw milk 2013). Table 1 reports more details about the use of the agricultural area in the dairy farm under investigation. Part of the farm agricultural land was utilized in a double cropping system (maize silage and Italian ryegrass hay).

For all the crops, the cultivation practices were divided in different phases: 1) soil preparation, 2) soil tillage and seeding, 3) crop growth, 4) product harvesting, 5) field-to-farm transport and 6) storage. Culti- vation practices hugely varied among the different crops in particular with regards to pesticide application, harvest operation and farm storage.
The Subsystem 2 (core processes, PCR, 2013) involves livestock management and feeding and milking procedures. The herd was com- posed mostly by Italian Friesian cows. Animals were kept in loose
Table 1 Utilization of the agricultural area in the dairy farm.

Cropping system|Crop|Area (ha)|Product Single crop|Maize hybrid 700|18.5|Whole plant silage Single crop|Maize hybrid 700|12.0|Ear silage "Double crop"|Maize hybrid 500|4.0|Whole plant silage "Double crop"|Italian ryegrass|4.0|Hay "Double crop"|Maize hybrid 500|4.5|Ear silage "Double crop"|Italian ryegrass|4.5|Hay Single crop|Winter cereals|5.0|Whole plant silage Single crop|Sorghum|6.0|Whole plant silage
housing condition, (cubicles, each with a mattress covered with chopped straw), without pasture, and milked twice a day.

The Subsystem 3 involves slurry storage. Cow slurry is stored in open tanks for 180 days before spreading on the fields during pre- seeding fertilization. During the storage period the slurry is mixed and emission of ammonia and methane occurs. Emissions due to slurry applications are involved in subsystem 1 because they depend on crop technique (sequence of field operation and, in particular, time between the application and soil incorporation), type of ma- chines and climatic conditions of the spreading day (temperature, wind, rainfall, etc.).

Cropping system|Crop|Area (ha)|Product Single crop|Maize hybrid 700|18.5|Whole plant silage Single crop|Maize hybrid 700|12.0|Ear silage "Double crop"|Maize hybrid 500|4.0|Whole plant silage "Double crop"|Italian ryegrass|4.0|Hay "Double crop"|Maize hybrid 500|4.5|Ear silage "Double crop"|Italian ryegrass|4.5|Hay Single crop|Winter cereals|5.0|Whole plant silage Single crop|Sorghum|6.0|Whole plant silage
housing condition, (cubicles, each with a mattress covered with chopped straw), without pasture, and milked twice a day.

The Subsystem 3 involves slurry storage. Cow slurry is stored in open tanks for 180 days before spreading on the fields during pre- seeding fertilization. During the storage period the slurry is mixed and emission of ammonia and methane occurs. Emissions due to slurry applications are involved in subsystem 1 because they depend on crop technique (sequence of field operation and, in particular, time between the application and soil incorporation), type of ma- chines and climatic conditions of the spreading day (temperature, wind, rainfall, etc.).
12: The goal of this study is to assess the environmental impact and to identify the environmental hotspots, as regard to the milk production system in Northern Italy. Besides this, the study is aimed to quantify the impact reduction arising from two different mitigations strategies: the introduction of a third daily milking and the anaerobic digestion (AD) of animal slurries.
For the milk production system, the environmental impacts were evaluated using the LCA methodology considering a cradle-to-farm gate perspective.
13: The system boundaries for the baseline scenario of milk production are shown in Fig. 1, as mentioned before this study has been carried out from a cradle-to-farm gate perspective.

The life cycle of each agricultural process was included within the system boundaries: raw materials extraction/production (e.g., fossil fuels and materials for purchased feed), manufacturing (e.g., seeds, fer- tilizers, pesticides, agricultural machines, processing animal feed), use (diesel fuel consumption and its combustion and tire abrasion emis- sions), maintenance of machines, and supply of inputs to the farm (e.g., fertilizers and herbicides).
In this study, the functional unit was established as 1 kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) leaving the farm gate, estimated using the formula (IDF, 2010):

In this study, the formula proposed by IDF was considered for its in- ternational acceptance and the recommendation to use a transforma- tion of milk quantity in fat and protein corrected milk was included in the main guidelines suggested by LEAP (2015).
14: The activities performed in the production system under study were identified by means of interviews and surveys on the farm (upstream data).

More specifically, for subsystem 1 (SS1 - on-farm crop production) information regarding fertilizers and pesticides was also collected by consulting the "farmer bookkeeper", a mandatory document in which their use must be reported. The biomass yields were measured by means of the farm weighbridge.

The agricultural field processes considered the characteristics (mass, power, life span, specific fuel consumptions, etc.) of the machines (tractors and implements) specifically used in the farm and taking into account the fuel consumptions recorded by means of surveys, farmer's interviews and direct measures (Table 2).

The diesel fuel consumption was estimated by using the model SE3A (Fiala and Bacenetti, 2012) that considers the power requirements of the operative machines and their work capacities. For the field opera- tions, a proper coupling among tractors and implements was always considered.

Emissions (nitrate, ammonia, and nitrous oxide) due to the appli- cations of nitrogen fertilizer (organic and mineral) were computed according to Brentrup et al. (2000) considering climatic data (e.g. temperature, wind, rainfall) and soil conditions (e.g. pH, texture, cat- ion exchange capacity). Climatic data for the year 2011, necessary for calculating emissions from fertilizers, were obtained from the

meteorological station closest to the experimental farm. Phosphate emissions were calculated following Prahsun (2006) and Nemecek and Kägi (2007). Table 3 reports the estimated emission to the envi- ronment for SS1. Pesticide derived emissions into water, soil and water were excluded from the system boundary due to the high un- certainty related to their fate in the environment (Sleeswijk et al., 2008; Fusi et al., 2014).

Background data for the production of seeds, diesel fuel, fertilizers, and pesticides as well as for off-farm activities were obtained from the Ecoinvent database (Althaus et al., 2007; Frischknecht et al., 2007; Jungbluth et al., 2007; Nemecek and Kägi, 2007; Spielmann et al., 2007).

For subsystem 2 (SS2 - livestock) the primary data about number of animals and herd composition, housing systems at the breeding farm and animal rations, milk and meat production and animal sold (number and live weight) were obtained through farmer interview (Table 5, baseline scenario). Data regarding all the inputs entering the farm were collected: amount of purchased feeds (both roughages and con- centrates), fertilizers and pesticides, bedding materials, number and or- igin of purchased replacing animals, electricity used for milking parlor and milk refrigeration.

Forages (hays and silages) and total mixed ration were sampled and analyzed for the content of dry matter, ash, crude protein and ether ex- tract by AOAC International (1995) methods; NDF analyzed following the protocol of Mertens (2002); and ADF and ADL by the method of Van Soest et al. (1991).
Data obtained from the analyses were used for the estimation of en- teric methane emission and to calculate feeding ration digestibility for estimation of methane emission from slurry.

The following off farm processes were considered: the production chain of commercial feed (from crop growing to feed factory process- ing), the production of purchased forages and bedding material, rearing of purchased replacing heifers and transportations.
Soybean meal was assumed to come from soybean grown in south- ern Brazil (as consequence no land-use change within the past 20 years) as suggested by Dourmad et al. (2014).

Methane (CH4) emissions from livestock enteric fermentations were estimated using an equation (equation number: 2d 12 = 0.63) from Ellis et al. (2007). Energy utilization for milking parlor, milk refrigerator, ventilation, lighting and barn cleaning was considered.

Animal nitrogen excretion was estimated as proposed by the IPCC (2006) Tier 2 method considering the nitrogen intake (on the basis of crude protein percentage of the diet) minus the nitrogen retained by the animals and excreted with milk.

For subsystem 3 (SS3) methane emissions from slurry storage before spreading were estimated using the Tier 2 method suggested by IPCC (2006). Nitrous oxide (N20) emissions from slurry storages occurred in direct and indirect forms and in both cases they were estimated using the Tier 2 method from IPCC (2006).

Ammonia (NH3) and nitrogen oxide emissions (NOx) that occur dur- ing animal housing (subsystem 2) and slurry storages (subsystem 3) were estimated following the method proposed by EEA (2009) on the basis of the total amount of nitrogen excreted by the animals. Emis- sion factors and more details about IPCC equations adopted were re- ported by Bava et al. (2014).

The fields employed for fodder production were previously dedicat- ed to cereal or forage cultivation. Therefore, no change in the overall soil carbon content was assumed (Dressler et al., 2012; Gan et al., 2011).
The amount of estimated emissions to the environment from SS1, SS2, and SS3 are reported in Tables 3 and 4.

Data obtained from the analyses were used for the estimation of en- teric methane emission and to calculate feeding ration digestibility for estimation of methane emission from slurry.

The following off farm processes were considered: the production chain of commercial feed (from crop growing to feed factory process- ing), the production of purchased forages and bedding material, rearing of purchased replacing heifers and transportations.
Soybean meal was assumed to come from soybean grown in south- ern Brazil (as consequence no land-use change within the past 20 years) as suggested by Dourmad et al. (2014).

Methane (CH4) emissions from livestock enteric fermentations were estimated using an equation (equation number: 2d 12 = 0.63) from Ellis et al. (2007). Energy utilization for milking parlor, milk refrigerator, ventilation, lighting and barn cleaning was considered.

Animal nitrogen excretion was estimated as proposed by the IPCC (2006) Tier 2 method considering the nitrogen intake (on the basis of crude protein percentage of the diet) minus the nitrogen retained by the animals and excreted with milk.

For subsystem 3 (SS3) methane emissions from slurry storage before spreading were estimated using the Tier 2 method suggested by IPCC (2006). Nitrous oxide (N20) emissions from slurry storages occurred in direct and indirect forms and in both cases they were estimated using the Tier 2 method from IPCC (2006).

Ammonia (NH3) and nitrogen oxide emissions (NOx) that occur dur- ing animal housing (subsystem 2) and slurry storages (subsystem 3) were estimated following the method proposed by EEA (2009) on the basis of the total amount of nitrogen excreted by the animals. Emis- sion factors and more details about IPCC equations adopted were re- ported by Bava et al. (2014).

The fields employed for fodder production were previously dedicat- ed to cereal or forage cultivation. Therefore, no change in the overall soil carbon content was assumed (Dressler et al., 2012; Gan et al., 2011).
The amount of estimated emissions to the environment from SS1, SS2, and SS3 are reported in Tables 3 and 4.
18: The activities performed in the production system under study were identified by means of interviews and surveys on the farm (upstream data).

More specifically, for subsystem 1 (SS1 - on-farm crop production) information regarding fertilizers and pesticides was also collected by consulting the "farmer bookkeeper", a mandatory document in which their use must be reported. The biomass yields were measured by means of the farm weighbridge.

The agricultural field processes considered the characteristics (mass, power, life span, specific fuel consumptions, etc.) of the machines (tractors and implements) specifically used in the farm and taking into account the fuel consumptions recorded by means of surveys, farmer's interviews and direct measures (Table 2).

The diesel fuel consumption was estimated by using the model SE3A (Fiala and Bacenetti, 2012) that considers the power requirements of the operative machines and their work capacities. For the field opera- tions, a proper coupling among tractors and implements was always considered.

Emissions (nitrate, ammonia, and nitrous oxide) due to the appli- cations of nitrogen fertilizer (organic and mineral) were computed according to Brentrup et al. (2000) considering climatic data (e.g. temperature, wind, rainfall) and soil conditions (e.g. pH, texture, cat- ion exchange capacity). Soybean meal was assumed to come from soybean grown in south- ern Brazil (as consequence no land-use change within the past 20 years) as suggested by Dourmad et al. (2014).

Methane (CH4) emissions from livestock enteric fermentations were estimated using an equation (equation number: 2d 12 = 0.63) from Ellis et al. (2007). Energy utilization for milking parlor, milk refrigerator, ventilation, lighting and barn cleaning was considered.

Animal nitrogen excretion was estimated as proposed by the IPCC (2006) Tier 2 method considering the nitrogen intake (on the basis of crude protein percentage of the diet) minus the nitrogen retained by the animals and excreted with milk.

For subsystem 3 (SS3) methane emissions from slurry storage before spreading were estimated using the Tier 2 method suggested by IPCC (2006). Nitrous oxide (N20) emissions from slurry storages occurred in direct and indirect forms and in both cases they were estimated using the Tier 2 method from IPCC (2006).

Ammonia (NH3) and nitrogen oxide emissions (NOx) that occur dur- ing animal housing (subsystem 2) and slurry storages (subsystem 3) were estimated following the method proposed by EEA (2009) on the basis of the total amount of nitrogen excreted by the animals. Emis- sion factors and more details about IPCC equations adopted were re- ported by Bava et al. (2014).

The fields employed for fodder production were previously dedicat- ed to cereal or forage cultivation. Therefore, no change in the overall soil carbon content was assumed (Dressler et al., 2012; Gan et al., 2011).
The amount of estimated emissions to the environment from SS1, SS2, and SS3 are reported in Tables 3 and 4.
22: The activities performed in the production system under study were identified by means of interviews and surveys on the farm (upstream data).

More specifically, for subsystem 1 (SS1 - on-farm crop production) information regarding fertilizers and pesticides was also collected by consulting the "farmer bookkeeper", a mandatory document in which their use must be reported. The biomass yields were measured by means of the farm weighbridge.

The agricultural field processes considered the characteristics (mass, power, life span, specific fuel consumptions, etc.) of the machines (tractors and implements) specifically used in the farm and taking into account the fuel consumptions recorded by means of surveys, farmer's interviews and direct measures (Table 2).

The diesel fuel consumption was estimated by using the model SE3A (Fiala and Bacenetti, 2012) that considers the power requirements of the operative machines and their work capacities. For the field opera- tions, a proper coupling among tractors and implements was always considered.

Emissions (nitrate, ammonia, and nitrous oxide) due to the appli- cations of nitrogen fertilizer (organic and mineral) were computed according to Brentrup et al. (2000) considering climatic data (e.g. temperature, wind, rainfall) and soil conditions (e.g. pH, texture, cat- ion exchange capacity). Climatic data for the year 2011, necessary for calculating emissions from fertilizers, were obtained from the

meteorological station closest to the experimental farm. Phosphate emissions were calculated following Prahsun (2006) and Nemecek and Kägi (2007). Table 3 reports the estimated emission to the envi- ronment for SS1. Pesticide derived emissions into water, soil and water were excluded from the system boundary due to the high un- certainty related to their fate in the environment (Sleeswijk et al., 2008; Fusi et al., 2014).

Background data for the production of seeds, diesel fuel, fertilizers, and pesticides as well as for off-farm activities were obtained from the Ecoinvent database (Althaus et al., 2007; Frischknecht et al., 2007; Jungbluth et al., 2007; Nemecek and Kägi, 2007; Spielmann et al., 2007).

For subsystem 2 (SS2 - livestock) the primary data about number of animals and herd composition, housing systems at the breeding farm and animal rations, milk and meat production and animal sold (number and live weight) were obtained through farmer interview (Table 5, baseline scenario). Data regarding all the inputs entering the farm were collected: amount of purchased feeds (both roughages and con- centrates), fertilizers and pesticides, bedding materials, number and or- igin of purchased replacing animals, electricity used for milking parlor and milk refrigeration.

Forages (hays and silages) and total mixed ration were sampled and analyzed for the content of dry matter, ash, crude protein and ether ex- tract by AOAC International (1995) methods; NDF analyzed following the protocol of Mertens (2002); and ADF and ADL by the method of Van Soest et al. (1991).
Data obtained from the analyses were used for the estimation of en- teric methane emission and to calculate feeding ration digestibility for estimation of methane emission from slurry.

For subsystem 3 (SS3) methane emissions from slurry storage before spreading were estimated using the Tier 2 method suggested by IPCC (2006). Nitrous oxide (N20) emissions from slurry storages occurred in direct and indirect forms and in both cases they were estimated using the Tier 2 method from IPCC (2006).

Ammonia (NH3) and nitrogen oxide emissions (NOx) that occur dur- ing animal housing (subsystem 2) and slurry storages (subsystem 3) were estimated following the method proposed by EEA (2009) on the basis of the total amount of nitrogen excreted by the animals. Emis- sion factors and more details about IPCC equations adopted were re- ported by Bava et al. (2014).

The fields employed for fodder production were previously dedicat- ed to cereal or forage cultivation. Therefore, no change in the overall soil carbon content was assumed (Dressler et al., 2012; Gan et al., 2011).
The amount of estimated emissions to the environment from SS1, SS2, and SS3 are reported in Tables 3 and 4.

26: The selected environmental impact categories were: global warming potential (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM), acidification potential (g SO2-eq/kg FPCM), eutrophication potential (g PO4 -eq/kg FPCM) and photochem- ical oxidation (g C2H4 eq/kg FPCM), energy use, MJ/kg FPCM). The im- pact assessment was performed with the EPD (2008) method, using SimaPro software (Prè Consultant, 2014); global warming potential was calculated according to the following 100-years factors expressed in kg CO2 equivalents, CO2: 1; CH4: 25, N20: 298 (IPCC, 2007). Energy use includes the use of energy from non-renewable sources.
27: Two mitigation strategies were applied to the baseline scenario (BS) in order to evaluate the effect of alternative management practices on environmental impact of milk production.

The third daily milking strategy (3MS) involved the transition from two to three milkings per day. In comparison with the baseline scenario, the different milking scheme entailed: an increase of 15% of milk yield per year according with Erdman and Varner (1995) and Smith et al. (2002); a decrease of percentages of fat 2.45%) and protein -0.64%) of milk and an increase of heifers and calves due to a higher culling rate as reported by Smith et al. (2002); an increase of farm elec- tricity use for the milking system -11.8%). Feed intake of milking COWS was not modified in 3MS in comparison with baseline scenario as re- ported by De Peters et al. (1985) and Barnes et al. (1990) and, more re- cently by Andersen et al. (2003) with an experiment with lactating cows in the first 16 weeks of lactation and by Hart et al. (2013) in a cross over experiment in the middle of lactating period. Detailed inven- tory data for 3 M strategy are shown in Table 5.

The second strategy involves the anaerobic digestion (AD) of the an- imal slurry in a consortium anaerobic digestion plant. The AD plant, fed with animal slurries coming also from nearby farms, has an electrical power of 300 kW (electric efficiency = 37.7%; thermal efficiency 45.0%, thermal power = 411 kW), it produces 2400 MWh of electricity per year (CHP engine working time = 8000 h/year) and it operates in mesophilic conditions (40 °C). For cow slurry, a biomethane specific production of 13.3 m³// (wet basis) has been assumed (Bacenetti et al., 2013). The distance between the dairy farm and the biogas plant has been fixed at 3 km; by means of a slurry tank, the animal slurries are daily transported to the AD plant and introduced into the digester, while, the digestate is transported back to the dairy farm where it is stored. The thermal energy cogenerated is partially used for heating the digester (35%) and partially wasted (about 65%) while the electricity produced by the AD plant is fed into the national electric grid. Electric self-consumptions, taken from the national grid, are equal to 10% of the produced electricity in according to previous studies focused on AD plants fed with animal slurry (Bacenetti et al., 2013; Lijó et al., 2014b). Considering that the digestate is stored in a covered tank, no emissions occur at this stage (Amon et al., 2006). Differently, the emis- sions related to digestate spreading have been taken into account and assessed following the same methods already described in paragraph 2.3 about organic fertilization. To this purpose, the following digestate composition has been considered: dry matter content = 5.13%; N = 0.40%; P2O5 = 0.08%, K20 = 0.31% (Bacenetti et al., 2013). The inputs considered for the AD plant are: (i) the transport carried out with a slur- ry tank coupled with a 90 kW tractor, (ii) the consumption of NaOH (0.25 kg/day) for the biogas treatment (desulphurization), (iii) the

electricity for pumps, mixers, devices for biogas treatment (e.g., chiller) and for biomass loading. As regard to the outputs, beside to the electric energy produced, the emissions related to biogas combustion were con- sidered. In more details, following NERI (2010), the emission in air of pollutants such as NOx, CO, NMVOC, and N20 as well as of unburned methane were taken into account.

The electricity generated by the AD plants avoids the generation of EE from other sources, therefore its environmental impact, evaluated considering the Italian electric mix (Ecoinvent, 2012), has been subtracted from the environmental burdens of the whole milk produc- tion system. The system boundaries of Subsystem 3 for the ADS are re- ported in Fig. 2.

electricity for pumps, mixers, devices for biogas treatment (e.g., chiller) and for biomass loading. As regard to the outputs, beside to the electric energy produced, the emissions related to biogas combustion were con- sidered. In more details, following NERI (2010), the emission in air of pollutants such as NOx, CO, NMVOC, and N20 as well as of unburned methane were taken into account.

The electricity generated by the AD plants avoids the generation of EE from other sources, therefore its environmental impact, evaluated considering the Italian electric mix (Ecoinvent, 2012), has been subtracted from the environmental burdens of the whole milk produc- tion system. The system boundaries of Subsystem 3 for the ADS are re- ported in Fig. 2.
30: Two mitigation strategies were applied to the baseline scenario (BS) in order to evaluate the effect of alternative management practices on environmental impact of milk production.

The third daily milking strategy (3MS) involved the transition from two to three milkings per day. In comparison with the baseline scenario, the different milking scheme entailed: an increase of 15% of milk yield per year according with Erdman and Varner (1995) and Smith et al. (2002); a decrease of percentages of fat 2.45%) and protein -0.64%) of milk and an increase of heifers and calves due to a higher culling rate as reported by Smith et al. (2002); an increase of farm elec- tricity use for the milking system -11.8%). Feed intake of milking COWS was not modified in 3MS in comparison with baseline scenario as re- ported by De Peters et al. (1985) and Barnes et al. (1990) and, more re- cently by Andersen et al. (2003) with an experiment with lactating cows in the first 16 weeks of lactation and by Hart et al. (2013) in a cross over experiment in the middle of lactating period. Detailed inven- tory data for 3 M strategy are shown in Table 5.

The second strategy involves the anaerobic digestion (AD) of the an- imal slurry in a consortium anaerobic digestion plant. The AD plant, fed with animal slurries coming also from nearby farms, has an electrical power of 300 kW (electric efficiency = 37.7%; thermal efficiency 45.0%, thermal power = 411 kW), it produces 2400 MWh of electricity per year (CHP engine working time = 8000 h/year) and it operates in mesophilic conditions (40 °C). For cow slurry, a biomethane specific production of 13.3 m³// (wet basis) has been assumed (Bacenetti et al., 2013). The distance between the dairy farm and the biogas plant has been fixed at 3 km; by means of a slurry tank, the animal slurries are daily transported to the AD plant and introduced into the digester, while, the digestate is transported back to the dairy farm where it is stored. The thermal energy cogenerated is partially used for heating the digester (35%) and partially wasted (about 65%) while the electricity produced by the AD plant is fed into the national electric grid. Electric self-consumptions, taken from the national grid, are equal to 10% of the produced electricity in according to previous studies focused on AD plants fed with animal slurry (Bacenetti et al., 2013; Lijó et al., 2014b). Considering that the digestate is stored in a covered tank, no emissions occur at this stage (Amon et al., 2006). Differently, the emis- sions related to digestate spreading have been taken into account and assessed following the same methods already described in paragraph 2.3 about organic fertilization. To this purpose, the following digestate composition has been considered: dry matter content = 5.13%; N = 0.40%; P2O5 = 0.08%, K20 = 0.31% (Bacenetti et al., 2013). The inputs considered for the AD plant are: (i) the transport carried out with a slur- ry tank coupled with a 90 kW tractor, (ii) the consumption of NaOH (0.25 kg/day) for the biogas treatment (desulphurization), (iii) the
Inventory data for SS 2 in the baseline scenario (BS) and 3 milking strategy (3MS).

32: Two sensitivity analyses were performed in order to understand how different assumptions affected the results. The hypotheses were 1) a variation + 20% of biogas production (Lijó et al., 2014a, Lijó et al., 2014b, Bacenetti et al., 2015a); and 2) an increase number of animals in the herd 2.5% in addition to scenario 3MS) necessary for shifting from 2 to 3 milkings/day.

The second hypothesis was based on data by Brotzman et al. (2015), the study reported an increasing of culling rate in herd with 3 milkings/day, that were struggling in some areas of manage- ment. This further enlargement of the herd forces in purchasing heifers from other farms; a simplified LCA was performed to estimate the envi- ronmental load of the purchased animals.
33: In the baseline scenario, the following environmental impact poten- tials are related to the production of 1 kg of FPCM divided for the three subsystem (as suggest by PCR V 1.01): global warming potential = 1.12 kg CO2 eq; acidification potential = 15.5 g SO2 eq; eutrophication
Fig. 2. System boundaries of Subsystem 3 in ADS 2. The dotted arrows indicate the energy self-consumption (thermal - TE: red; electric - EE: blue).
potential = 5.62 g PO eq; photochemical oxidation potential = 0.87 g C2H4 eq/kg FPCM; energy use = 4.66 MJ eq (Table 6).
These results are similar to the values found by other authors for milk production in Italian intensive dairy farms (Guerci et al., 2013; Bava et al., 2014; Battini et al., 2014).

The main share of the environmental burdens are due to Subsystem 2 (Fig. 3), even with differences among impact categories; in particular global warming potential, photochemical oxidation potential and ener- gy use were mainly due to livestock subsystem (69, 86 and 74%, respectively).

Also the on-farm crop production (Subsystem 1) is an environmen- tal hotspot of the system representing a remarkable part of acidification potential (28.8%), eutrophication potential (29.3%) and energy use (25.7%) while it is lower for global warming potential.

Fig. 4 reports the contribution of the different activities and inputs to the environmental burdens of Subsystem 1 (on-farm crop production). In the figure, the impacts of agricultural machinery operations were gathered, along with the ones for fertilizers and agro-chemical produc- tions and emissions, in air (N20, NH3) and in water asso- ciated with fertilizer applications. For Subsystem 1, the hotspots are agricultural machinery operations and field emissions. Field emissions play a predominant role in three of the five evaluated impact categories. For acidification and eutrophication potential, the emissions associated with fertilizer applications (leaching, nitrous oxide production, and vol- atilization) had a deep impact (over 92%). Agricultural machinery oper- ations are responsible for the main share of the environmental load for impact categories such as global warming potential (49.8%) and energy use (89.1%). The results obtained regarding the impact of the field oper- ations suggest that, for this aspect, the diesel fuel consumption plays a key role. In fact, the machinery field operations (e.g., plowing and har- vesting) with the higher environmental impacts are characterized by high fuel consumption.

Fertilizer production is responsible for the 8.4% for the energy use impact category while it has a low impact over the other impact catego- ries. To this regard, it has to be highlighted that for the different crops the use of mineral fertilizers is limited to top fertilization while the pre-seeding one is performed with organic fertilizers. Seed production as well as the pesticide one have a little impact on the environmental burdens of SS1 (lower than 3%). As regard to these results, it must be underlined that the impact categories evaluated in this study are only slightly affected by pesticide application. Nevertheless, other studies
Environmental impact values for baseline scenario (BS) and the two mitigation strategies (third milking = 3MS and anaerobic digestion = ADS).

highlighted that, for the toxicity-related impact categories, application of plant protection products must be carefully evaluated (Capri and Karpouzas, 2007; Sleeswijk et al., 2008).
The Fig. 5 shows the contribution to the different impact categories of inputs and activities for the Subsystem 2 (livestock).

As expected, for global warming potential the most important contribution were slurry and enteric emissions (60.6%) due to meth- ane produced by enteric fermentation and nitrogen gases derived by slurry in the barn, as reported by Misselbrook et al. (2013). Pur- chased feed (both roughages and concentrates) loaded for the 32% of global warming potential of the livestock subsystem and generat- ed 46.7% of the total CO2 emitted in the three subsystems. Such a high contribution is mainly due to the emission of CO2 during con- centrate production and transportation and is explained by the low level of feed self-sufficiency of the farm (63.3%). The largest quota of feed purchased were raw materials and compound concentrate feed (1.63 t DM/LU per year for the two items), Baek et al. (2014) showed that feed with high gross energy content are at the same time the feed with the highest contribution to global warming po- tential. Purchased feed played also an important role on eutrophica- tion potential, energy use. Emissions from enteric fermentation and from slurry in barn gave a great contribution to AP both considering only the livestock subsystem (73.3%, Fig. 5) and considering all the three subsystems (60%). Farm energy use, related to milking parlor and milk storage, gave a limited contribution to all environmental categories with the exception of energy use (30.1%).

Subsystem 3 involves slurry storage (but not the slurry spreading that it is involved in SS1). Cow slurry is stored in open tanks for 180 days before being spread on the fields during pre-seeding fertiliza- tion as organic fertilizer. During the storage, the slurry is mixed and emissions of ammonia and methane occur.

These emissions do not affect ozone layer depletion and energy use and they only slightly influence the photochemical oxidation (3.7%). On the other hand, the impact of methane and ammonia emissions cannot be neglected for global warming (19.6%), acidification (29.2%) and eu- trophication (18.3%).

highlighted that, for the toxicity-related impact categories, application of plant protection products must be carefully evaluated (Capri and Karpouzas, 2007; Sleeswijk et al., 2008).
The Fig. 5 shows the contribution to the different impact categories of inputs and activities for the Subsystem 2 (livestock).

As expected, for global warming potential the most important contribution were slurry and enteric emissions (60.6%) due to meth- ane produced by enteric fermentation and nitrogen gases derived by slurry in the barn, as reported by Misselbrook et al. (2013). Pur- chased feed (both roughages and concentrates) loaded for the 32% of global warming potential of the livestock subsystem and generat- ed 46.7% of the total CO2 emitted in the three subsystems. Such a high contribution is mainly due to the emission of CO2 during con- centrate production and transportation and is explained by the low level of feed self-sufficiency of the farm (63.3%). The largest quota of feed purchased were raw materials and compound concentrate feed (1.63 t DM/LU per year for the two items), Baek et al. (2014) showed that feed with high gross energy content are at the same time the feed with the highest contribution to global warming po- tential. Purchased feed played also an important role on eutrophica- tion potential, energy use. Emissions from enteric fermentation and from slurry in barn gave a great contribution to AP both considering only the livestock subsystem (73.3%, Fig. 5) and considering all the three subsystems (60%). Farm energy use, related to milking parlor and milk storage, gave a limited contribution to all environmental categories with the exception of energy use (30.1%).

Subsystem 3 involves slurry storage (but not the slurry spreading that it is involved in SS1). Cow slurry is stored in open tanks for 180 days before being spread on the fields during pre-seeding fertiliza- tion as organic fertilizer. During the storage, the slurry is mixed and emissions of ammonia and methane occur.

These emissions do not affect ozone layer depletion and energy use and they only slightly influence the photochemical oxidation (3.7%). On the other hand, the impact of methane and ammonia emissions cannot be neglected for global warming (19.6%), acidification (29.2%) and eu- trophication (18.3%).
36: In the baseline scenario, the following environmental impact poten- tials are related to the production of 1 kg of FPCM divided for the three subsystem (as suggest by PCR V 1.01): global warming potential = 1.12 kg CO2 eq; acidification potential = 15.5 g SO2 eq; eutrophication
Fig. 2. System boundaries of Subsystem 3 in ADS 2. The dotted arrows indicate the energy self-consumption (thermal - TE: red; electric - EE: blue).
potential = 5.62 g PO eq; photochemical oxidation potential = 0.87 g C2H4 eq/kg FPCM; energy use = 4.66 MJ eq (Table 6).
These results are similar to the values found by other authors for milk production in Italian intensive dairy farms (Guerci et al., 2013; Bava et al., 2014; Battini et al., 2014).

The main share of the environmental burdens are due to Subsystem 2 (Fig. 3), even with differences among impact categories; in particular global warming potential, photochemical oxidation potential and ener- gy use were mainly due to livestock subsystem (69, 86 and 74%, respectively).

Also the on-farm crop production (Subsystem 1) is an environmen- tal hotspot of the system representing a remarkable part of acidification potential (28.8%), eutrophication potential (29.3%) and energy use (25.7%) while it is lower for global warming potential.

Fig. 4 reports the contribution of the different activities and inputs to the environmental burdens of Subsystem 1 (on-farm crop production). In the figure, the impacts of agricultural machinery operations were gathered, along with the ones for fertilizers and agro-chemical produc- tions and emissions, in air (N20, NH3) and in water asso- ciated with fertilizer applications. For Subsystem 1, the hotspots are agricultural machinery operations and field emissions. Field emissions play a predominant role in three of the five evaluated impact categories. For acidification and eutrophication potential, the emissions associated with fertilizer applications (leaching, nitrous oxide production, and vol- atilization) had a deep impact (over 92%). Agricultural machinery oper- ations are responsible for the main share of the environmental load for impact categories such as global warming potential (49.8%) and energy use (89.1%). The results obtained regarding the impact of the field oper- ations suggest that, for this aspect, the diesel fuel consumption plays a key role. In fact, the machinery field operations (e.g., plowing and har- vesting) with the higher environmental impacts are characterized by high fuel consumption.

Fertilizer production is responsible for the 8.4% for the energy use impact category while it has a low impact over the other impact catego- ries. To this regard, it has to be highlighted that for the different crops the use of mineral fertilizers is limited to top fertilization while the pre-seeding one is performed with organic fertilizers. Seed production as well as the pesticide one have a little impact on the environmental burdens of SS1 (lower than 3%). As regard to these results, it must be underlined that the impact categories evaluated in this study are only slightly affected by pesticide application. Nevertheless, other studies

Fig. 2. System boundaries of Subsystem 3 in ADS 2. The dotted arrows indicate the energy self-consumption (thermal - TE: red; electric - EE: blue).
potential = 5.62 g PO eq; photochemical oxidation potential = 0.87 g C2H4 eq/kg FPCM; energy use = 4.66 MJ eq (Table 6).
These results are similar to the values found by other authors for milk production in Italian intensive dairy farms (Guerci et al., 2013; Bava et al., 2014; Battini et al., 2014).

The main share of the environmental burdens are due to Subsystem 2 (Fig. 3), even with differences among impact categories; in particular global warming potential, photochemical oxidation potential and ener- gy use were mainly due to livestock subsystem (69, 86 and 74%, respectively).

Also the on-farm crop production (Subsystem 1) is an environmen- tal hotspot of the system representing a remarkable part of acidification potential (28.8%), eutrophication potential (29.3%) and energy use (25.7%) while it is lower for global warming potential.

Fig. 4 reports the contribution of the different activities and inputs to the environmental burdens of Subsystem 1 (on-farm crop production). In the figure, the impacts of agricultural machinery operations were gathered, along with the ones for fertilizers and agro-chemical produc- tions and emissions, in air (N20, NH3) and in water asso- ciated with fertilizer applications. For Subsystem 1, the hotspots are agricultural machinery operations and field emissions. Field emissions play a predominant role in three of the five evaluated impact categories. For acidification and eutrophication potential, the emissions associated with fertilizer applications (leaching, nitrous oxide production, and vol- atilization) had a deep impact (over 92%). Agricultural machinery oper- ations are responsible for the main share of the environmental load for impact categories such as global warming potential (49.8%) and energy use (89.1%). The results obtained regarding the impact of the field oper- ations suggest that, for this aspect, the diesel fuel consumption plays a key role. In fact, the machinery field operations (e.g., plowing and har- vesting) with the higher environmental impacts are characterized by high fuel consumption.

Fertilizer production is responsible for the 8.4% for the energy use impact category while it has a low impact over the other impact catego- ries. To this regard, it has to be highlighted that for the different crops the use of mineral fertilizers is limited to top fertilization while the pre-seeding one is performed with organic fertilizers. Seed production as well as the pesticide one have a little impact on the environmental burdens of SS1 (lower than 3%). As regard to these results, it must be underlined that the impact categories evaluated in this study are only slightly affected by pesticide application. Nevertheless, other studies

highlighted that, for the toxicity-related impact categories, application of plant protection products must be carefully evaluated (Capri and Karpouzas, 2007; Sleeswijk et al., 2008).
The Fig. 5 shows the contribution to the different impact categories of inputs and activities for the Subsystem 2 (livestock).

As expected, for global warming potential the most important contribution were slurry and enteric emissions (60.6%) due to meth- ane produced by enteric fermentation and nitrogen gases derived by slurry in the barn, as reported by Misselbrook et al. (2013). Pur- chased feed (both roughages and concentrates) loaded for the 32% of global warming potential of the livestock subsystem and generat- ed 46.7% of the total CO2 emitted in the three subsystems. Such a high contribution is mainly due to the emission of CO2 during con- centrate production and transportation and is explained by the low level of feed self-sufficiency of the farm (63.3%). The largest quota of feed purchased were raw materials and compound concentrate feed (1.63 t DM/LU per year for the two items), Baek et al. (2014) showed that feed with high gross energy content are at the same time the feed with the highest contribution to global warming po- tential. Purchased feed played also an important role on eutrophica- tion potential, energy use. Emissions from enteric fermentation and from slurry in barn gave a great contribution to AP both considering only the livestock subsystem (73.3%, Fig. 5) and considering all the three subsystems (60%). Farm energy use, related to milking parlor and milk storage, gave a limited contribution to all environmental categories with the exception of energy use (30.1%).

Subsystem 3 involves slurry storage (but not the slurry spreading that it is involved in SS1). Cow slurry is stored in open tanks for 180 days before being spread on the fields during pre-seeding fertiliza- tion as organic fertilizer. During the storage, the slurry is mixed and emissions of ammonia and methane occur.

These emissions do not affect ozone layer depletion and energy use and they only slightly influence the photochemical oxidation (3.7%). On the other hand, the impact of methane and ammonia emissions cannot be neglected for global warming (19.6%), acidification (29.2%) and eu- trophication (18.3%).
41: Table 6 shows the environmental impact potentials and the on-farm contribution of each impact in the two mitigation strategies. All data

refers on 1 kg of FPCM. For global warming potential, acidification po- tential and eutrophication potential on farm activities covered more than fifty percentage of total impacts, as observed also by Bava et al. (2014).

The increase of milking frequency led to better results for all impact categories in comparison with the Baseline Scenario; in particular acid- ification potential and eutrophication potential showed the largest re- ductions (from 11 to 12%; Fig. 6). The reduction of global warming potential was close to 10%. Third milking strategy compared with base- line scenario implies an increase of feed efficiency due to the higher milk yield at constant feed intake. Flysjö et al. (2011) reported that milk yield and feed intake are two of the most influential parameters in carbon footprint estimates, indicating that feed efficiency (expressed as unit of corrected milk produced/unit DMI) can be used as a rough key performance indicator of measures to reduce milk carbon footprint on farm level. The mitigation effect of enhancing dairy efficiency is based on the dilution of environmental costs associated with maintenance (Bava et al., 2014). Zehetmeier et al. (2014) focused the attention on milk yield and replacement rate, as the most important variables explaining variation of GHG emissions; because milk yield/cow of high performing farms is already in the upper range, the focus in these farms should be on an optimal combination of milk yield and replace- ment rate to further mitigate GHG emissions. The advantage of 3MS strategy in terms of GWP was moderate, due to methane emission from an higher number of replacing animals, as a consequence of the higher culling rate; this slightly reduce the positive effect of higher milk production.
The energy use was the impact category that showed the lower ad- vantage with the increase of milking frequency, as a consequence of in- creased operating time of the milking equipment.

From the results of Table 6, the anaerobic digestion of cow slurry is confirmed as an effective strategy to mitigate the environmental impact of milk production at farm level. In AD strategy, compared to the baseline one, the impact for four of the five assessed impact categories was lower thanks to the reduction of emissions of meth- ane and ammonia that usually take place during slurry storage in open lagoons/tanks. The highest reductions were achieved for the impact categories more affected by these emissions: acidification po- tential (-29%), global warming potential -22%) and eutrophica- tion potential 18%). The reduction of the load for the energy use impact category was about of 15% and it was completely due to the
Fig. 3. Contribution of three subsystems to environmental impact categories in the production of 1 kg of FPCM in the baseline scenario.
Fig. 4. Contribution of inputs and activities on impact categories of subsystem 1 in the baseline scenario.

credits for the electricity production by biogas. These credits offset the environmental load due to the transportation of slurry and digestate between the farm and the biogas plant. For photochemical oxidation potential the impact reduction is really little; for this im- pact category the credits arising electricity production from biogas are slightly lower that the environmental effects related to the emis- sions from the CHP engine.

With regard to ADS the achieved results are in according to previous studies that highlighted that AD of animal waste (slurry and manure) is an effective solution in order to reduce the environmental burdens of dairy products (De Boer et al., 2011; Kröbel et al., 2013; Battini et al., 2014) and/or to produce sustainable renewable energy (Bacenetti et al., 2013; Lijó et al., 2014a; Lijó et al., 2014b).

The sensitivity analysis that has been carried out on the specific bio- gas production with a variation of + 20% of this parameter confirmed the robustness of the achieved results for the ADS. In fact, when the minimum or maximum range values were considered, only variations lower than 1% were observed for the environmental impact of the FU. The most affected impact category was energy use; its impact decreased of 0.66% with the maximum specific biogas production, while grows of 0.63% if the minimum value was considered.

The second sensitive analysis, changing the percentage of heifers in the herd, showed small effects on environmental categories in compar- ison with the 3MS scenario. In particular, the mitigation effect of 3MS decreased from 3.32% for eutrophication potential to 0.75% for energy use. A mitigation loss of 3.24% was registered for global warming
Fig. 5. Contribution of inputs and activities on impact categories of Subsystem 2 in the baseline scenario.
J. Bacenetti et Science of the Total Environment 539 (2016) 450-459
Fig. 6. Comparison among the three different alternative strategies (BS = baseline scenario; 3MS = third milking strategy; ADS = anaerobic digestion strategy).
potential. These results confirm that a good management is needed changing milking frequency to obtain a robust mitigation effect.

credits for the electricity production by biogas. These credits offset the environmental load due to the transportation of slurry and digestate between the farm and the biogas plant. For photochemical oxidation potential the impact reduction is really little; for this im- pact category the credits arising electricity production from biogas are slightly lower that the environmental effects related to the emis- sions from the CHP engine.

With regard to ADS the achieved results are in according to previous studies that highlighted that AD of animal waste (slurry and manure) is an effective solution in order to reduce the environmental burdens of dairy products (De Boer et al., 2011; Kröbel et al., 2013; Battini et al., 2014) and/or to produce sustainable renewable energy (Bacenetti et al., 2013; Lijó et al., 2014a; Lijó et al., 2014b).

The sensitivity analysis that has been carried out on the specific bio- gas production with a variation of + 20% of this parameter confirmed the robustness of the achieved results for the ADS. In fact, when the minimum or maximum range values were considered, only variations lower than 1% were observed for the environmental impact of the FU. The most affected impact category was energy use; its impact decreased of 0.66% with the maximum specific biogas production, while grows of 0.63% if the minimum value was considered.

The second sensitive analysis, changing the percentage of heifers in the herd, showed small effects on environmental categories in compar- ison with the 3MS scenario. In particular, the mitigation effect of 3MS decreased from 3.32% for eutrophication potential to 0.75% for energy use. A mitigation loss of 3.24% was registered for global warming

47: The environmental impact potential of milk production in Northern Italy has been assessed though a cradle to farm gate LCA. In the study, the environmental load due to the crop cultivation (5 different crops grown under two different cropping systems) has been evaluated in de- tail considering site-specific data directly collected in an experimental farm. Two different mitigation strategies have been evaluated (increase of milking frequency and anaerobic digestion).

Livestock management and feeding and milking procedures are re- sponsible of most of the environmental impact of milk production, in particular for impact categories such as global warming potential, pho- tochemical oxidation potential and energy use. On-farm crop produc- tion plays a key role for acidification potential, eutrophication potential and energy use; as for the impact of manure management, acidification potential cannot be neglected.

The increase of milking frequency (from 2 to 3 milkings per day) al- lows a reduction for all the evaluated impact categories, mainly thanks to increased milk production per cow. This solution could lead also eco- nomic advantages to farmers, considering that it does not need any structural investment. The results for this scenario are based on the hy- pothesis that the ration is adequate for such a milk production growth; if not, the benefits arising from the third milking might have been reduced.

Anaerobic digestion of slurry has proven to be an effective solution to mitigate the environmental burdens of milk production, in particular for GWP and acidification impact categories. Especially for GWP, the im- pact reduction of the anaerobic digestion scenario is remarkable and is due to the reduction of methane emissions from slurry storage as well as to credits for electricity production, which substitutes fossil electrici- ty. As regard to acidification, the anaerobic digestion of slurry allows a significant improvement of the environmental performance, mainly be- cause ammonia emissions from slurry storage are considerably reduced.

The recent revision of the Italian subsidy framework for electricity generation from biogas has reduced the subsidies and the possibility to feed the AD plants with energy crops. As a consequence, only AD plants fed with animal slurry and agricultural by-products can be prof- itable. Nevertheless, it should be considered that these feedstock usually have a low specific biogas production and a low energy density. In this context, the hypothesis to carry out the anaerobic digestion of animal slurry in a consortium plant close to several dairy farms is a solution that counters with the reduction of the tariffs and, concurrently, im- proves the environmental performances of all the dairy farms involved.
Finally, it must be consider that the two mitigation strategies evalu- ated in this study could be adopted together summing up the environ- mental benefits.
